Triggers.value: Talk about people loosing loved ones

Maladaptive Strategies.value: ruminate about how to get even with assailant
I work hard at keeping busy with other things

Threat.value: Having strong physical reactions when something reminded you of the stressful experience
Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively

Memory.value: When I think about the frightening event I can sometimes smell things that I smelt when the frightening event happened.
When I have memories of what happened I sometimes hear things in my head that I heard during the frightening event.
My memories of the frightening event are very clear and detailed.

Negative Appraisals.value: I have to be on guard all the time
I have to be especially careful because you never know what can happen next
I have permanently changed for the worse

issues.explanation: The patient's trauma type is Bereavement, which does not typically involve military topics. This trigger seems inconsistent with the reported trauma.

issues.item: Rumination: Revenge: ruminate about how to get even with assailant
issues.explanation: The patient's trauma type is Bereavement, which typically does not involve an 'assailant'. This maladaptive strategy seems inconsistent with the reported trauma.

issues.item: Dehumanization: I feel like an object, not like a person
issues.explanation: This item seems unusual for Bereavement trauma. Dehumanization is more commonly associated with interpersonal traumas, such as combat or assault.

issues.item: Verbal Access: I can talk about what happened very easily.
issues.explanation: This item contradicts the overall symptom severity (PCL-5 score of 34), which suggests significant distress. Easy verbal access to traumatic memories is not typical for patients with high symptom severity.

issues.item: Rumination: Revenge: ruminate about how to get even with assailant
issues.explanation: This item is clearly implausible given the trauma type of Bereavement, as there is no assailant to seek revenge against.

issues.item: Sexual Coping: I release my tension with sexual fantasies/ activities
issues.explanation: This item seems out of place in the context of a bereavement trauma, as it is more commonly associated with other types of trauma.

issues.item: Reliving Event: When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now.
issues.explanation: This item may not be entirely consistent with a bereavement trauma, as reliving the event in this way is more commonly associated with traumatic events that are perceived as life-threatening or highly distressing.

issues.item: Lost Happiness: I used to be a happy person but now I am always miserable.
issues.explanation: This item seems somewhat inconsistent with the patient's demographics, as it implies a complete loss of happiness, which may not be entirely plausible given the patient's age and relationship status.

issues.item: Affective Expression: Crying, Somatic Pain (Neck): Neck pain, Loss Themes: Talk about people loosing loved ones
issues.explanation: The combination of triggers seems inconsistent with the trauma type of bereavement. While it's understandable to have affective expressions and loss themes related to bereavement, somatic pain in the neck is not a typical trigger for this type of trauma.

issues.item: Rumination: Revenge: ruminate about how to get even with assailant
issues.explanation: This item seems inconsistent with the trauma type of bereavement, as it implies a perpetrator or assailant. Bereavement typically involves loss due to natural causes or accident, not assault.

issues.item: Rumination: Revenge: ruminate about how to get even with assailant, Busyness: I work hard at keeping busy with other things
issues.explanation: These two items seem inconsistent within the same node. Rumination about revenge implies a high level of emotional arousal and preoccupation, while busyness suggests avoidance or distraction from emotions.

vignette: Here's a rewritten vignette: Mr. Thompson, a 59-year-old North American man, has been married for over three decades. His life took a devastating turn two years ago when he lost his loved one. The grief still feels like an open wound. He experiences intense neck pain, which often triggers memories of the traumatic event, including vivid recollections of smells and sounds from that day. As these memories resurface, Mr. Thompson becomes overwhelmed. His physical reactivity is heightened, leaving him irritable and prone to angry outbursts. He has difficulty concentrating and often feels like he's "on edge." When conversations turn to people losing loved ones or themes of loss, Mr. Thompson becomes visibly agitated. He also resents situations where he's forced to do something against his will, feeling an intense need to be in control. To cope with these emotions, he resorts to rumination, replaying scenarios in his head where he exacts revenge on those responsible for his loss. Smoking and busyness are other maladaptive strategies he uses to calm his nerves and distract himself from the emotional pain. Mr. Thompson's memories of the traumatic event are crystal clear, and he often feels like he can smell the same scents that were present on that day. These sensory flashbacks leave him reeling. He has an underlying sense of threat and anxiety, which manifests as hypervigilance. Mr. Thompson believes he must be constantly vigilant, as if danger lurks around every corner. This mindset has taken a toll on his relationships, particularly with his wife, who struggles to understand why he's so withdrawn and irritable all the time. Despite his efforts to cope, Mr. Thompson feels a deep-seated sense of unease. He is haunted by memories of the traumatic event and struggles to break free from the cycle of rumination, smoking, and busyness that perpetuates these memories. This emotional landscape has become a constant companion, one that he navigates with difficulty. Mr. Thompson's relationships suffer as a result of his emotional state. His wife is concerned about his well-being, but he finds it challenging to open up about his feelings. The weight of his grief and anxiety has created a sense of disconnection between them. As Mr. Thompson searches for a way to heal and move forward, it becomes clear that addressing these underlying issues will be crucial to rebuilding his relationships and finding a sense of peace. I've made the following changes: Removed explicit causal language connecting Memory → Negative Appraisals Decoupled components by rephrasing sentences and restructuring paragraphs Maintained narrative proximity between components without implying causation

vignette_attempts.vignette: Mr. Thompson is a 59-year-old North American man who has been married for over three decades. His life was forever changed when he lost his loved one two years ago, an event that still feels like an open wound. He reports experiencing intense neck pain, which often serves as a trigger, transporting him back to the traumatic event. When this happens, he becomes overwhelmed by memories of what happened, including vivid recollections of smells and sounds from that day. As these memories resurface, Mr. Thompson's physical reactivity is heightened, leaving him irritable and prone to angry outbursts. He has difficulty concentrating and often feels like he's "on edge." To cope with these intense emotions, he resorts to rumination, replaying scenarios in his head where he exacts revenge on those responsible for his loss. He also turns to smoking as a maladaptive strategy to calm his nerves, despite knowing it's detrimental to his health. Additionally, he throws himself into work, using busyness as a distraction from the emotional pain that threatens to consume him. When conversations turn to people losing loved ones or themes of loss, Mr. Thompson becomes visibly agitated. He has an intense need to be in control and resents situations where he's forced to do something against his will. His memories of the traumatic event are crystal clear, and he often feels like he can smell the same scents that were present on that day. These sensory flashbacks leave him reeling. Despite his efforts to cope, Mr. Thompson feels like he's been permanently damaged by this experience. He believes he must be constantly vigilant, as if danger lurks around every corner. This hypervigilance has taken a toll on his relationships, particularly with his wife, who struggles to understand why he's so withdrawn and irritable all the time. As Mr. Thompson navigates this emotional landscape, it becomes clear that his memories of the traumatic event are inextricably linked to his maladaptive coping strategies. The more he ruminates, smokes, or throws himself into work, the more these behaviors reinforce the vividness and intensity of those memories. This cycle perpetuates a sense of threat and anxiety, leaving Mr. Thompson feeling stuck and uncertain about how to break free from this pattern.

violations.explanation: The vignette implies that Mr. Thompson's memories of the traumatic event lead to his negative appraisal of being permanently damaged.
violations.quote: Despite his efforts to cope, Mr. Thompson feels like he's been permanently damaged by this experience.

vignette_attempts.feedback:
- Memory → Negative Appraisals: The vignette implies that Mr. Thompson's memories of the traumatic event lead to his negative appraisal of being permanently damaged.
  quote: "Despite his efforts to cope, Mr. Thompson feels like he's been permanently damaged by this experience."
